title: The Fear of the Lord
reading:
Every other Spirit described in Isaiah 11 has a function. The Fear of the Lord has a disposition. It is not what you do — it is the posture from which everything else flows.

The fear of the Lord is not terror of an angry God. It is the appropriate response of a creature standing before the Creator — a holy reverence that takes God seriously enough to obey Him, honor Him, and refuse to treat Him casually.

When Abraham raised the knife over Isaac, he was not performing for an audience. He was responding to the God he had come to know — a God whose commands he had learned to trust even when they made no sense. The angel's words confirmed it: "Now I know that you fear God, since you have not withheld your son, your only son, from me" (Genesis 22:12). The fear of the Lord produces obedience that goes beyond what is comfortable.

Moses at the burning bush removed his sandals without being asked twice. He hid his face. He trembled. This was not weakness — this was the correct response of a man who had encountered the holy. The fear of the Lord calibrates your responses. It keeps you from treating sacred things casually.

Isaiah's vision in chapter 6 undoes him. He sees God high and lifted up, the seraphim covering their faces and feet, the temple filled with smoke. His first word is "Woe is me." He does not begin with a request. He begins with an honest recognition of what he is in the presence of what God is. That recognition is the beginning of wisdom — and of usefulness. After the coal touches his lips, he hears the call: "Whom shall I send?" and answers: "Here I am, send me."

The early church walked "in the fear of the Lord and in the comfort of the Holy Spirit" (Acts 9:31). These were not opposites. The fear of the Lord was not oppressive — it was clarifying. It kept the church holy, and holiness kept the church powerful.
reflection:
How would you describe your current posture toward God — is there genuine reverence, or have you become casual in your approach to Him?

What is one area of your life where the fear of the Lord would produce different choices than you are currently making?

How does Isaiah 6 change the way you approach worship, prayer, or Scripture reading?
abide: Lord, restore in me a right fear of You. Not dread — but reverence. The kind that takes You seriously. The kind that refuses to treat holy things casually. Where I have become familiar in a way that borders on careless, forgive me and restore proper weight to who You are. Let the fear of the Lord be the foundation of everything I do.
